claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child or children you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - just start with the first child's age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 18 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're welcome, I'm happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is **18 years old**, he falls into the **16 to 19 age category**. For this age group, there are additional requirements to check around education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For young people aged 16-19 like Alex, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their education or training status.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **In education or training** - Alex is currently enrolled in a course or approved training programme
2. **Recently left education or training** - Alex has left education or training within the last few months
3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Please let me know which applies to Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Alex is currently studying for his A-levels. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! A-levels are on the list of **approved qualifications** for Child Benefit purposes. I just need to confirm a few more details about Alex's studies:

**A-levels are approved education**, but I need to check:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/lessons)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer, or does Alex have an agreement with an employer for a job after the course?** (If so, this would affect eligibility)

Could you confirm these details for me?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is studying full-time - he's at sixth form college doing his A-levels. And no, there's no employer involved - it's just regular college education.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Alex's education fully meets the requirements:

- ✅ **A-levels** - an approved (non-advanced) qualification
- ✅ **Full-time study** - at sixth form college
- ✅ **No employer funding** involved

Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's work situation.

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week (or not have a paid job at all)?**

This is important because young people aged 16+ who work 24 or more hours per week may not qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's focused on his studies full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great - Alex is fully focused on his studies.

- ✅ **Not working 24+ hours per week** - Alex doesn't have a paid job

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question about Alex:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own name?**

Specifically, I need to check whether Alex is claiming any of the following benefits in his own right:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

Is Alex free from receiving these benefits himself?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex does receive Universal Credit in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. Unfortunately, this affects Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, I've reached a conclusion about Alex's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Child Benefit Eligibility Result for Alex

### ❌ Not Eligible

I'm sorry, but **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

### Why?

Although Alex meets many of the requirements for Child Benefit:
- ✅ Aged 16-19 (he's 18)
- ✅ In approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college)
- ✅ No employer involvement in his course
- ✅ Not working 24+ hours per week

**However**, because Alex receives **Universal Credit in his own name**, he is no longer considered a "qualifying child" for Child Benefit purposes.

### The Rule

When a young person aged 16-19 claims certain benefits in their own right (including Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance), Child Benefit cannot be claimed for them. This is because receiving these benefits recognises them as independent for benefit purposes.

### What You Can Do

- If Alex's circumstances change in the future (e.g., he stops claiming Universal Credit), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for him again while he remains in approved education and under 20
- If you have any other children, you may still be eligible to claim Child Benefit for them

---

Is there anything else I can help you with, or do you have any other children you'd like me to check eligibility for?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally qualify him for Child Benefit. However, Alex receives Universal Credit in his own name. Under Child Benefit rules, when a young person aged 16-19 claims certain benefits in their own right (including Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance), they are no longer considered a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes. This means the claimant cannot receive Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Although Alex meets the education requirements (full-time A-level study, non-advanced qualification, no employer involvement, not working 24+ hours per week), he is disqualified because he receives Universal Credit in his own name.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.reason: Child is receiving certain benefits in their own right
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child starts receiving Employment and Support Allowance, Universal Credit, or similar benefits in their own right.

This means the claimant cannot receive Child Benefit for Alex.
